Platform & Data.narrative: This family ensures your manufacturing data is accessible, secure, and connected to other business systems. Singapore SMEs need this to let shop-floor staff, planners, and managers access production information from any device—and to comply with data protection regulations like PDPA. A typical scenario: your production manager checks real-time output on a mobile phone during a site visit, while your accountant exports costing data to reconcile with the general ledger.

Product Engineering.narrative: This family captures what you make (bills of materials), how you make it (routings and work centers), and how you manage changes to designs and recipes. Singapore SMEs rely on this to avoid costly production errors, speed up new product launches, and maintain traceability for quality audits. For example: when a customer requests a product variant, you formally change the BOM and routing, and the system automatically updates all future work orders.

Production Execution.narrative: This family turns your engineering plans into real production—creating work orders, scheduling them on the shop floor, and capturing what actually happened. SMEs need this to meet delivery dates, optimize machine utilization, and reduce manual paperwork on the factory floor. A concrete example: your scheduler assigns a batch to the injection-molding line for Tuesday morning, operators scan materials and log output in real time, and the system flags delays before they impact the next job.

Quality & Compliance.narrative: This family embeds quality checks, traceability, and safety rules into every production step. Singapore SMEs must have this to pass customer audits, meet regulatory requirements (especially in food, pharma, and electronics), and quickly isolate defective batches. For instance: if a customer reports a faulty unit, you instantly trace it back to the raw material lot and production batch, then forward-trace all related products to determine the scope of recall.

Planning & Costing.narrative: This family forecasts demand, calculates what materials and capacity you need, and tracks the true cost of what you make. SMEs depend on this to avoid stockouts and overstock, meet customer deadlines, and understand profitability by product. A typical scenario: your planner runs MRP to see that a surge in orders will exhaust raw material in two weeks, triggering an early purchase order; simultaneously, the costing engine shows that a recent price increase has eroded margins on one product line.

Maintenance & Analytics.narrative: This family monitors machine health, calculates overall equipment effectiveness (OEE), and handles repairs and rework. SMEs need this to minimize unplanned downtime, spot performance trends, and make data-driven decisions about equipment investment. For example: your OEE dashboard reveals that a packaging line has 15% downtime due to changeovers; you use that insight to negotiate a maintenance contract or justify a faster changeover tool.
